title_en: Supplication and Remembrance: Theological and Practical Dimensions
content_en:
Al-Adhkar contains important theological and practical discussions of supplication and remembrance alongside the specific formulas it presents. An-Nawawi devoted sections of the book to discussing the spiritual reality of du'a — the supplication through which the believer communicates directly with God — including the conditions for its acceptance, the etiquette of its performance, and the prophetic guidance on the times and states when it is most likely to be answered. These sections give Al-Adhkar a depth that distinguishes it from a mere anthology of formulas.

The discussion of the virtues of dhikr — the remembrance of God in its various forms — is one of the theological highlights of Al-Adhkar. An-Nawawi gathered traditions that describe the spiritual reality of remembering God: the traditions that speak of the remembrance of God as the best of deeds, as a light in the heart of the believer, as a protection from the devices of Shaytan, and as the primary means by which the heart is polished and brought into proximity with its Creator. These discussions situate the specific formulas of the adhkar within a broader theology of the human relationship with the divine.

The sections on the Quran as dhikr are among the most important in Al-Adhkar. An-Nawawi gathered the prophetic traditions on the special status of Quranic recitation as a form of remembrance, the protective power of specific Surahs and verses, and the spiritual benefits of regular engagement with the Quran. These sections connect Al-Adhkar to the broader tradition of Quran-centered spirituality that has been central to Islamic devotional practice across cultures and centuries.

The sections on salawat — invoking God's blessings on the Prophet — are particularly beloved by readers of Al-Adhkar. An-Nawawi gathered the prophetic formulas for invoking blessings on the Prophet and discussed the spiritual significance and legal status of this practice. The tradition that the one who invokes blessings on the Prophet once will receive ten blessings from God has made the salawat a central feature of Muslim devotional life, and Al-Adhkar's treatment of this practice has helped to preserve and transmit it across generations.
